title: The Value of Differing Points of View: Evidence from Financial Analysts’ Geographic Diversity
abstract: Using satellite imagery of retail firms’ parking lots to measure time-varying local firm-specific performance, we document that analysts incorporate local information into their forecasts. Analysts rely more on local signals when less firm-wide information is available. This incorporation of noisy local firm information has firm-level implications. Examining across industries, we find causal evidence that geographic concentration of analysts increases consensus forecast errors and decreases firm liquidity. These effects are stronger for harder-to-value stocks. The market values geographic firm information, as the abnormal return around forecast revisions is higher for analysts who cover a firm from a unique location.

article.1. Data:
JEL D82 - Asymmetric and Private Information; Mechanism Design G14 - Information and Market Efficiency; Event Studies; Insider Trading G41 - Role and Effects of Psychological, Emotional, Social, and Cognitive Factors on Decision Making in Financial Markets Issue Section: Article Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Well-functioning markets require participants to deliver a diverse set of information about assets in that market (Hayek 1945). Some types of information in financial markets are disseminated widely and available uniformly (e.g., annual reports and conference calls), whereas other types of information may be geographically dispersed and not equally accessible to all market participants (Van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp 2009). As the typical public firm has operations across several states, firm-specific information is not solely concentrated at corporate headquarters (Bernile, Kumar, and Sulaeman 2015). Geographically dispersed market participants can speak with the local employees, local customers, and local suppliers of these widely spread firms. They may glean information from their local media or have close personal ties with local executives that provide them with unique firm-specific information. In contrast, the equity research industry in the United States, which serves as an important information intermediary, is quite geographically concentrated, raising questions about the industry’s ability to gather and incorporate dispersed firm-specific information. Whether diverse information available to participants is distributed widely or concentrated can have important implications for the market (Goldstein and Yang 2015). In this paper, we ask whether equity analysts incorporate geographically dispersed firm-specific information into financial markets.

Studying how dispersed firm-specific information is incorporated into markets can be challenging as this information is typically difficult to observe.1In this paper, we take advantage of satellite image data that provide us with parking lot car counts of retail firms across different metropolitan areas over time to examine the dispersion of information in the setting of retail sell-side analysts. Analysts, especially analysts covering retail firms, often highlight firm-specific local information like store visits in their primary research.2We combine these data with information on the location of sell-side analysts from a series of Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) requests for work histories of brokerage employees to provide us with a measure of time-varying, firm-specific local information to which analysts may be exposed.

Using this detailed data for a sample of retail firms from 2009 to 2015, we confirm that signals of local firm-specific performance (i.e., parking lot car counts observed using satellite data) contain valuable information about overall firm performance at an aggregate level. The activity at retail stores is an important signal of overall firm performance, but for any single analyst in a particular forecasting period this local signal of overall performance is received with idiosyncratic noise. We take advantage of the fact that multiple analysts are trying to forecast thesameevent during thesameperiod but withdifferentexposures to local information to control for common factors and exploit the idiosyncratic nature of local signals to understand its influence. Further, as analysts typically cover multiple firms in the same area, we can separate firm-specific information from common time-varying local and macroeconomic shocks.

For example, consider three financial analysts working in New York, Minneapolis, and Cleveland, respectively, who each make a quarterly earnings forecast for Home Depot in Q1 of 2013. While each analyst could access the same SEC filings and listen to the same conference calls, the analyst in Minneapolis may observe full parking lots at local Home Depot locations in Minneapolis and infer that the firm is doing well overall, while the analyst in Cleveland may observe mostly empty parking lots in Cleveland and make the opposite inference. While retail analysts may actually observe parking lots and make inferences from them, local firm-specific conditions also may be passed through other means, such as interactions with local employees, managers, customers, or suppliers. As analysts are located in different locations and the satellite data allows us to observe local firm conditions at these locations at the same point in time, we can control for common information available to all analysts during the quarter. Moreover, because analysts typically cover multiple firms and the satellite data are available for each firm in the locale, we can remove any common “Minneapolis” effect for the analyst (e.g., by examining Minneapolis area performance of other firms covered by the analyst, such as Lowe’s or Walmart) even if this local effect varies over time by including MSA|$\times$|YQ fixed effects.

We document that retail firm analysts incorporate local firm-specific information (i.e., firm performance for stores near the analyst) into their quarterly earnings estimates. Using a series of fixed effects specifications, we are able to rule out many competing stories that arise because of common information, such as company filings, firm characteristics, matching between analyst characteristics and the firms that they cover, and differences in time-varying MSA characteristics like general local economic conditions. In terms of statistical magnitude, our preferred specification suggests that a one-standard-deviation increase in a firm’s local car counts relative to the national average is associated with a 44.6-basis-point (bp) increase in an analyst’s quarterly earnings forecast relative to the actual earnings number, a 5.6|$\%$|deviation from the mean. As a check of this specification, we generate a bootstrapped sample distribution by randomly reassigning car count differences from other firms within an MSA. The distribution is centered around zero suggesting that the MSA|$\times$|YQ fixed effects do a good job of eliminating any influence from local economic performance and our main results are unlikely to occur by chance. Moreover, this effect is stronger when less firmwide information is available to analysts. Forecasts early in the forecast period weight local firm-specific information more heavily than later in the same period when more other information is available. Similarly, we find analysts assign less weight to local firm-specific information in the presence of more common information, such as SEC filings.

We then turn to implications for the consensus forecast and firm liquidity. While analysts may rationally weight local information as this is their best guess of national trends, analysts may have difficulty in identifying the idiosyncratic component of their local signal in real time.3If analysts systematically incorporate noise from local information into their forecasts, then we would expect to see the crowd forecast to average away these errors when the consensus is made up of a geographically diverse population—and correlated errors to remain when they are not.

This is precisely what we find. The influence of local firm performance on geographically dispersed individual analysts’ forecasts has important implications for the formation of consensus forecasts. When analysts are clustered within a single locale, their individual forecasts tend to be shaded in the same direction and result in a relatively higher forecast error. In contrast, when analysts are geographically diverse, the influence of these varied exposures offset, reducing consensus forecast error. Using a broader sample of firms in the IBES database, we find evidence that consensus forecast errors are larger when analysts are more concentrated in fewer metropolitan areas (i.e., less geographically diverse). Therefore, documenting that the geographic diversity of analysts affects the accuracy of consensus forecasts has important implications for the efficiency of asset prices. Consistent with our results in the retail sample regarding the importance of geographic information in the presence of other information, we document these effects are stronger among harder-to-value stocks, which typically have less firmwide information available. We find the impact of geographic diversity is stronger for firms that are smaller, younger, have lower stock prices, higher idiosyncratic volatility, and higher skewness.

We employ within-firm panel regressions to show that increased geographic diversity is associated with lower consensus forecast errors. Although we have no obvious reasons to believe that endogeneity would be an important concern in this setting, as brokerage firms are unlikely to shift their entire research staffs’ operations in response to their relative geographic dispersion for a single stock they cover, we nonetheless exploit the exogenous shift in geographic coverage due to brokerage closures and mergers. We expand on the approach used by prior work, such asHong and Kacperczyk (2010)andKelly and Ljungqvist (2012), by looking within firms that lose analyst coverage due to the exogenous reduction in analyst coverage. In these tests, we find larger increases in consensus forecasts errors in firms that see reduced geographic coverage (e.g., a firm loses its only analyst who is located in Minneapolis) relative to firms that also lose analyst coverage but retain the same geographic diversity (e.g., a firm with three analysts in the New York City metropolitan area loses one of these three New York analysts). These findings suggest the increased forecast errors that result from brokerage closures stem from not just the reduction in competition between analysts, but also the loss of geographic diversity.

As analysts are vital to the production of market-related information, their lack of geographic diversity may have spillover effects in financial markets. In particular, the information environment may be worse for firms with less geographic coverage, which could lead to a decrease in stock liquidity (Harford et al. 2019). Consistent with this conjecture, we find bid-ask spreads around earnings announcements are larger for firms with less geographically diverse analysts. This finding is robust to the inclusion of|${\it Firm}$|fixed effects and|${\it Number\ of\ analyst}$|fixed effects and is stronger for harder-to-value stocks. Further, using the exogenous reduction in analyst coverage from brokerage closures and mergers setting, we find higher spreads for firms that experience a loss in geographic coverage, suggesting the results are causal.4

Next, we examine whether investors value geographic diversity in analyst coverage. Exploiting an analyst’s geographic status relative to peers covering the same firm, we examine market reactions to forecast and recommendation revisions. We find that the market reaction to forecast revisions is 9|$\%$|–13|$\%$|stronger when the revision is made by geographically unique analysts (i.e., the only analyst covering a certain firm from a particular MSA) relative to analysts who are in an area with multiple analysts covering the same firm. This suggests that geographically unique analysts deliver forecasts that contain distinct information on average. Notably, the results are consistent when including|${\it Analyst}$|fixed effects, ruling out alternative explanations like differences in analyst quality.

Our final tests document that the information content in stock prices is affected by analyst geographic dispersion. Theoretical models likeGoldstein and Yang (2015)suggest that a firm’s stock price will be more informative about future cash flows when the information that investors can access is more diverse. We test this theory in our setting using a future earnings response coefficient model and find the relationship between current stock returns and future firm earnings is stronger for firms that are covered by geographically dispersed analysts. This finding implies that analyst geographic dispersion is an important component of information diversity and can influence the informational efficiency of stock prices.

A firm’s information environment is influenced by a variety of sources, including public news (Boudoukh et al. 2019), modern information technologies (Gao and Huang 2020), analysts’ access to management (Green et al. 2014), and analysts’ effort allocation (Harford et al. 2019), among others. Our study specifically contributes to a growing literature on the importance of geographically dispersed information about firms. While much of the existing literature has focused on firm headquarters as the sole source of firm information (e.g.,Chen 2017;Coval and Moskowitz 2001;Malloy 2005), the typical public firm has operations in multiple states (Bernile, Kumar, and Sulaeman 2015;Garcia and Norli 2012).Garcia and Norli (2012)extract state name counts from 10-K filings and show important differences in the information environment among firms with business operations concentrated in a few states from firms with operations in multiple states.Addoum, Kumar, and Law (2020)follow a similar approach and provide evidence that the market is slow in aggregating state-level information in geographically diverse firms. One key advantage of our setting over simply looking at states to which a firm may be exposed is that we observefirm-specificsignals of geographically dispersed (but local to the analyst) information. While actual parking lot car counts may be one type of information, in and of itself, for retail firms, it may also proxy for these other related forms of firm-specific local information (such as those mentioned inCoval and Moskowitz (2001), talking with suppliers and employees, visiting operations, or private information due to access to local corporate managers). Thus, forecasts for a given financial analyst, who is provided with various local signals and provides multiple outputs (forecasts), can be compared to forecasts made by other analysts who are receiving different signals about the same firm at the same time. This allows us to use high-dimensional fixed effects to control for unobservable factors that confound analysis at a more aggregated level.

We also contribute to the understanding of how analysts form forecast predictions.Brown et al. (2015)provide survey evidence that attempts to understand the context within which analysts make their decisions. More than half of analysts view primary research, such as channel checks, as “very useful,” which is consistent with our empirical findings. Two recent studies use the geography of analysts to try to understand how analysts learn from each other.Chen, Mayew, and Yan (2021)examine how social interaction between analysts influences forecast quality and find that the analysts who work in the same office transmit important information to each other when these analysts cover different firms that are headquartered in the same location.Cen, Chang, and Dasgupta (2020)also study the analyst location setting but focus on trying to infer whether analysts appear to learn from other geographically dispersed forecasts. Unlike the data used by these papers, the satellite data we use allow us to measure local firm-specific information, so we can employ high dimensional fixed effects to understand how time-varying local firm-specific information apart from other factors (such as learning from peers) affects analyst forecasts. Moreover, those studies aim to understand peer learning effects among analysts, while ours controls for such learning and instead focuses on how analyst geographic diversity affects how firm-specific information is incorporated into markets. Our study also relates toMerkley, Michaely, and Pacelli (2020), who examine a different dimension of diversity, ethnic surname diversity. They document a correlation between ethnic surname diversity and consensus forecast error. We show that the effects of geographic diversity are novel and unique, as we find that our results for geographic diversity hold even among stocks covered by analysts of a single ethnicity. Our study also connects the analysis of the satellite data setting with the broader set of firms in the IBES data, allowing us to show that analyst geographic diversity has implications for market efficiency, which is to our knowledge a novel result.

Finally, our paper contributes to the burgeoning literature that uses big and alternative data to study questions in finance.5Froot et al. (2017)use proprietary real-time measures of consumer activity and show that managers downplay current performance to take advantage of insider trading opportunities.Zhu (2019)studies the release of credit card transaction and satellite imagery data to investors and finds the dissemination of this data disciplines firms’ long-term investment decisions.Katona et al. (2021)also study the dissemination of satellite imagery but focus on the playing field between investors and show that sophisticated investors become better off while individual investors are hindered.Kang, Stice-Lawrence, and Wong (2021)show that local institutional investors appear to trade in the same direction as local parking lot counts. We contribute to this literature by demonstrating how alternative data can be used to create granular local information measures that allow for the study of open questions in finance and economics.
1.1 Analyst locations

Our data on financial analyst locations comes from historical filings of the Uniform Application for Securities Industry Registration or Transfer (Form U4), which provides detailed accounts of analyst registrations and work histories including the street address of office location. Form U4 is filed by an employer when the analyst joins the firm and must be updated upon material changes, such as changing jobs. The Form U4 data are aggregated in a database called the Central Registration Depository (CRD), which is jointly operated by FINRA and state securities regulators.

We obtain Form U4 data from a series of Freedom of Information Act requests to state regulators. Our universe of financial analysts consists of those registered in any of the states that respond to our requests during some point of their career. Analysts may register in multiple states, so we have data for many analysts in the states that do not supply information. We do not observe location data only for those analysts who never register in a reporting state. Although our sample of office locations is not comprehensive, it covers all major financial centers. As the selection mechanism for inclusion into the sample is a state regulator’s interpretation of the Privacy Act of 1974 as it relates to the FOIA request, selection is unlikely to systematically bias the correlation between our variables of interest that are time-varying within MSAs.

To match the analyst locations to earnings recommendations, we link the location data to IBES using a combination of name and career history matching. First, we match firm identifiers between the CRD database and IBES database. We then match individuals using a combination of first initial, last name, and career history. We drop observations with ambiguous information, such as missing or multiple names, in the name fields. Because career histories are present in both databases, we can take advantage of the timing of job switches to make matches when name strings are ambiguous (e.g., despite many “L. Smith” entries in both databases, only one in each database moves from Citigroup to Sutro & Co. in 1998 allowing us to make a distinct match). Further we can verify the quality of the matches, by checking their career start and end dates and FINRA licensing required of analysts. Research analysts must pass either the Series 86 or Series 87 exam to register as a research analyst. The certification allows the analyst to prepare written or electronic communications that analyze equity securities, companies, and industry sectors.

Consistent with prior studies (e.g.,Malloy 2005), we find that the slight majority (57.5|$\%$|⁠) of analysts are located in the New York metropolitan area. Analysts typically remain in the same MSA throughout our sample period with only 5|$\%$|switching MSAs. In panel A ofFigure 1, we display the geographic dispersion of analysts in the retail sample, along with the store locations of the retail firms. The size of the circle is proportional to the number of unique analysts in the sample. Outside of New York, several major geographically dispersed metropolitan areas, such as Chicago, Minneapolis, and San Francisco, each contain a sizable number of analysts. Further, numerous smaller metropolitan areas like Cleveland, Atlanta, Nashville, Boston, Dallas, and Washington have a meaningful headcount of analysts. We also show the location of analysts and stores for an example firm, Home Depot, in panel B ofFigure 1. Home Depot’s analyst coverage highlights the importance of geographic diversification if analysts look to incorporate local information. Though Home Depot’s analysts are relatively dispersed, many stores still have no local analyst coverage.

InTable 1, we report that the vast majority of firms have analysts covering the firm from multiple MSAs. However, most brokerage firms concentrate their analysts in a single location and only a handful have large staffs at multiple MSAs. This agglomeration pattern is consistent with the financial research industry having significant localized industry spillovers that dominate the potential information advantages of having dispersed analysts (Ellison and Glaeser 1999).

1.2 Parking lot data

To measure local firm performance, we use satellite imagery of daily parking lot car counts for major U.S. retail firms. We obtain data on parking lot car counts from Orbital Insight, a leading image processing company that uses machine learning to convert satellite images into quantitative data.6The data include the company name and ticker, a unique identifier for each store location, the latitude and longitude of the store, the date and time the image was taken, and a count of the number of cars in the parking lot when the image was taken. Orbital Insight provides a normalized measure based on the raw car count data that accounts for the day of the week, time of day the satellite image is taken, and the maximum number of cars a parking lot can likely hold. This normalization is based on empirically validated predictable patterns in daily retail traffic.7Similarly, Orbital Insight’s machine vision technology can detect and account for weather anomalies like cloud coverage. Our sample for daily car counts begins in January 2009 and ends in December 2015.

The daily car count data include 5.4 million parking lot observations covering 176,935 unique store locations for the 162 retailers covered by Orbital Insight. From the daily data, we create a measure of local firm performance by taking the average car count of a firm’s parking lots located in the same MSA during a given quarter. 8 We then create the variable |${\it Car\ count}\ \mathit{difference}$|⁠ , measured as $$\begin{equation}
\small
\textit{Car}\ \textit{count}\ \mathit{difference}_{i,m,q} =
\frac{\textit{Avg}\ \textit{local}\ \textit{car} \ \textit{count}_{i,m,q} - \textit{Avg}\ \textit{national} \ \textit{car}\ \textit{count}_{i,q}}
{\textit{Avg}\ \textit{national} \ \textit{car} \ \textit{count}_{i,q}},
\end{equation}$$ (1) where we construct |${\it Avg\ local \ car \ count}_{i,m,q} $| by first taking the average daily car count of each parking lot observed in MSA |$ m $| for firm |$ i $| and then averaging that measure across all days in quarter |$q$|⁠ . |${\it Avg\ national \ car \ count}_{i,q}$| is the average daily car count for firm |$i$| during quarter |$q$| across all observed store locations in the United States. |${\it Car\ count}\ \mathit{difference}$| therefore measures how well a firm performed in a given MSA relative to how the firm performed overall during a given quarter. By scaling by the national count, the variable has a natural interpretation as percentage difference. 9 Previous studies (e.g., Katona et al. 2021 ; Zhu 2019 ) have found that aggregate satellite data measures like |${\it Avg\ national \ car \ count}_{i,q}$| strongly predict firms’ future earnings per share. In contrast, |${\it Avg\ local \ car \ count}_{i,m,q} $| should have little predictive power for firm-level performance beyond the aggregate measure. By looking at the abnormal number of cars in a local MSA relative to the national average, we are able to isolate the noisy local information from the relevant aggregate information.

We then match the satellite data to the analyst location data at the firm, analyst-MSA, and quarter levels. The unison of these two data sets allows us to gauge the relative performance of a firm that an analyst is exposed to in a given quarter, which will vary both over time and in the cross-section for analysts in different MSAs. We also merge the data with CRSP and Compustat to obtain stock return and firm accounting data. We drop 24 firms that do not have analyst coverage or do not have stock return and accounting information in CRSP or Compustat. The final sample contains 18,072 quarterly observations for 402 analysts covering 138 firms.

To test whether analysts are influenced by the local performance of a firm, we analyze the relationship between a firm’s |${\it Car\ Count}\ \mathit{Difference}$| in an analyst’s MSA and that analyst’s forecast error. We measure forecast error as $$\begin{equation}
\textit{Error}_{i,j,q} = \frac{\textit{Forecast}_{i,j,q} - \textit{Actual}_{j,q}}{\textit{Price}_{j,q-4}}*100,
\end{equation}$$ (2) where |${\it Forecast}_{i,j,q}$| is analyst |$i$| ’s most recent forecast for firm |$j$| ’s earnings per share in quarter |$q$|⁠ , |${\it Actual}_{j,q}$| is firm |$j$| ’s actual earnings per share in quarter |$q$|⁠ , and |${\it Price}_{j,q-4}$| is firm |$j$| ’s stock price at the end of quarter |$q-4$|⁠ .

We examine summary statistics for the merged sample in the first panel ofTable 1. Analyst forecasts tend to be pessimistic, as reflected in the average error of|$-$|0.08. The average|${\it Car\ Count}\ \mathit{Difference}$|is 17|$\%$|⁠, suggesting stores of firms located near analysts tend to outperform the firm’s national average. There is large variation in|${\it Car\ Count}\ \mathit{Difference}$|⁠, with a standard deviation of 46|$\%$|⁠. There continues to be variation in|${\it Car\ Count}\ \mathit{Difference}$|within a firm-quarter, which we exploit in the design of our tests. This variation can arise because of several factors, including changes in local competition, weather, local advertising campaigns, and variation in regional management quality of a firm. For this study, we focus less on how this variation emerges and more on how the variation influences financial analysts’ information set.

The location of firm’s stores in our sample are widespread, as the average firm has store locations in 58|$\%$|of MSAs. This suggests some difficulty in gaining a complete picture of the performance of these stores solely based on the local performance in a single MSA. In a given quarter, forecasts are issued by 6.4 analysts for the average firm and those analysts are located in an average of 3.8 MSAs. Thus, despite the geographic diversity of firms, analysts tend to cluster in a few MSAs. We also note that the geographic concentration of analysts varies even within the largest firms in our sample (e.g., the 31 analysts that cover Walmart do so from five unique MSAs, while the 29 analysts that cover Home Depot do so from eight unique MSAs). We further document that 14|$\%$|of forecasts in our sample are made by analysts who do not live near a store of the firm they are forecasting.10The average analyst has approximately ten years of overall experience, 4.25 years of firm-specific experience, and issues forecasts two months before the earnings announcement. Firms in our sample have an average share turnover over the prior year of 0.27, market equity of $9.9 billion, and a book to market ratio of 0.42.

In panel B ofTable 1, we examine the summary statistics for the broader sample of all IBES analysts. We analyze the relation between the geographic concentration of analysts covering a specific firm and that firm’s consensus forecast error and bid-ask spread. We construct the consensus analyst forecast error as the price-scaled absolute error between the consensus forecast and the actual and the spread as the closing bid-ask spread scaled by the midpoint for the 3 days surrounding a firm’s earnings announcement. We calculate the MSA Herfindahl index using the count of analysts in each MSA in each firm-quarter to a market share by MSA which is then squared and summed across all MSAs in the firm-quarter. We multiply this index by negative one so that it may be interpreted as a dispersion index. The average consensus forecast error is 0.28, the average bid-ask spread is 0.61, and the average MSA dispersion index is|$-$|0.6. Contrary to the perception that all analysts are on Wall Street, we find that less than a third (30.7|$\%$|⁠) of firms have analysts that are all located in the same MSA. The majority of firms (69.3|$\%$|⁠) have analysts in two or more MSAs and 17.5|$\%$|of firms have analysts in five or more MSAs.

When comparing the analyst and firm characteristics between the satellite data sample and the full IBES sample, we find that firms in the satellite data sample generally have more experienced analysts, higher turnover, higher market value, and lower book to market values. The difference in characteristics between the two samples should not affect the validity of our findings for three reasons. First, our tests examining the satellite data sample are done at the firm-quarter level, so all firm-level characteristics (including time-varying ones) are subsumed. Second, we also employ specifications including|${\it Analyst} \times {\it Firm}$|and|${\it Analyst} \times {\it YQ}$|fixed effects to show that the same analyst’s forecast for the same firm will be influenced based on whether that firm’s local stores perform better or worse than the firm’s national performance in a given quarter. Third, in our later tests regarding consensus forecasts and liquidity, we find consistent results in both the satellite and full IBES samples, suggesting differences in firm and analyst characteristics do not bias our results.
article.2. Predicting Returns with Retail Store:
In this section, we begin by documenting that car count information (at least in aggregate) contains value relevant information. Several recent papers (e.g.,Katona et al. 2021;Zhu 2019) have documented the investment value of satellite data at the aggregated firm level. We follow their approach and extend the analysis to disaggregated local (i.e., MSA-firm level) observations to examine the return predictability of car counts for earnings announcement returns.

Local car counts for a particular firm are highly correlated but much noisier than the firm’s aggregated national car count. When a firm has high car counts nationwide, it obviously in aggregate has higher counts across the various local stores. However, idiosyncratic local conditions, such as weather, local management, local competition, or local economic conditions, can affect individual localities differently at any point in time. By definition, these idiosyncratic local components average out across the national measures. To illustrate this idea, we plot the relationship between local car counts and national car counts for an example firm, Home Depot, inFigure 2. Panel A demonstrates that observed local car counts are quite varied, suggesting it would be difficult to accurately predict national performance using only a single store’s car count. However, the binscatter plot in panel B shows that on average, when local car counts are higher, aggregate national car counts are higher.

Figure 2 National versus local car counts This figure presents scatterplots of national versus local car counts for an example firm in our data. Panel A offers a scatterplot of all local car counts versus the aggregate national count within a firm-quarter. Panel B provides a binscatter plot of the relative local car count (relative to county average for the firm) versus the relative aggregate national count (relative to the firm’s overall average). Throughout the majority of our sample period, direct access to real-time car count data was limited. Instead, analysts likely observe local realizations of store car counts (or correlated information like interactions with local managers or exposure to local advertising), along with national level information which is available to all analysts. We examine return predictability using the following two models: $$\begin{align}
\textit{Announcement}\ \textit{Return}_{j,q} &=
\beta_1 \textit{log}(\textit{Avg}\ \textit{National}\ \textit{Car}\ \textit{Count})_{j,q} \nonumber\\
&\quad +\, \beta_2 \textit{log}(\textit{Firm}\ \textit{Avg}\ \textit{Local}\ \textit{Car}\ \textit{Count})_{j,q} \nonumber\\
&\quad +\, \sum\limits_{k}\beta_k \textit{Firm}\ \textit{controls}_{j,q} + \Gamma FE + \epsilon_{j,q},\\
\end{align}$$ (3) $$\begin{align}
\textit{Announcement}\ \textit{Return}_{j,q} &= \beta_1 \textit{log}(\textit{Avg}\ \textit{National}\
\textit{Car}\ \textit{Count})_{j,q} \nonumber\\
&\quad +\, \beta_2 \textit{log}(\textit{Firm-MSA}\ \textit{Avg}\ \textit{Local}\ \textit{Car}\ \textit{Count})_{j,q,m} \nonumber\\
&\quad +\, \sum\limits_{k}\beta_k \textit{Firm}\ \textit{controls}_{j,q} + \Gamma \textit{FE} + \epsilon_{j,q,m},
\end{align}$$ (4) where Equation ( 3 ) is at the firm-quarter level and Equation ( 4 ) is at the MSA-firm-quarter level. |${\it Announcement\ Return}_{j,q}$| is firm |$j$| ’s stock return from the day of an earnings announcement until 3 days after. 11 |${\it log}({\it Avg\ National\ Car\ Count})_{j,q,}$| is the logarithm of firm |$j$| ’s average national car count during quarter |$q$|⁠ , |${\it log}({\it Firm\ Avg\ Local\ Car\ Count})_{j,q}$| is the average car count for firm |$j$| in all MSAs in which the analysts covering the firm live during quarter |$q$|⁠ , and |${\it log}({\it Firm-MSA\ Avg\ Local\ Car\ Count})_{j,q,m}$| is the logarithm of the average local car count in MSA |$m$| which analyst |$j$| observes during quarter |$q$|⁠ . Equation ( 3 ) allows us to analyze whether analysts could collectively use local firm information to aid in forecasting earnings, and Equation ( 4 ) allows us to exploit the variation in local firm information between MSAs. 12 We include firm controls for turnover (the logarithm of the number of shares traded divided by the number of shares outstanding over the prior 12 months), size (the logarithm of the market capitalization of the firm), book to market (the logarithm of book value of equity minus the logarithm of the market value of equity), and past return (the return of firm |$j$| from 30 days prior to announcement until 3 days prior to the announcement). We also control for the four-quarter lag of |${\it log}({\it Avg\ National\ Car\ Count})_{j,q,}$| in Equation ( 3 ). We include both |${\it Firm}$| and |${\it YQ}$| (i.e., |${\it Year} \times {\it Quarter}$|⁠ ) fixed effects and cluster standard errors at the firm-quarter level in Equation ( 3 ) and the MSA level in Equation ( 4 ).

Table 2shows the results for our return prediction tests. First, columns 1 and 4 show a significantly strong positive relationship between national car counts and announcements returns, which is consistent with the prior literature. We analyze the predictability of local car counts in columns 2 and 5 and find mixed evidence for the informativeness of local car counts. The coefficient for the local car count variable is positive in both models, but the magnitude is much smaller and is statistically insignificant in the firm-quarter model (column 2). When including the national and local car counts in the same model, we find that the national car count measure is still significant while the local signals are at best marginally useful. Thus, analysts may glean some useful information from local firm signals, but these signals will be subject to noise. The key implication fromFigure 2andTable 2is that local car counts contain relevant information, primarily because they are correlated with national car counts.

article.3. Evidence of Incorporation of Local Si:
3.1 Main results

In this section, we conduct a series of tests with which to examine how the local signals of a firm’s performance affects individual analysts’ forecasts. We employ a variety of fixed effect specifications which allow us to rule out any plausible alternative explanations. Our initial regression tests examine the satellite data sample using the following specification: $$\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\textit{Error}_{i,j,q} = & \beta_1 \textit{Car}\ \textit{count}\ \mathit{difference}_{i,j,q} +
\sum\limits_{n}\beta_n \textit{Analyst}\ \textit{controls}_{i,j,q} \\
& + \sum\limits_{k}\beta_k \textit{Firm}\ \textit{controls}_{j,q} + \Gamma \textit{FE} + \epsilon_{i,j,q},
\end{split}
\end{equation}$$ (5) where |${\it Error}_{i,j,q}$| is the price-scaled forecast error of analyst |$i$| for firm |$j$| in quarter |$q$| and |${\it Car\ count}\ \mathit{difference}_{i,j,q}$| is the relative local performance of firm |$j$| in the MSA of analyst |$i$| during quarter |$q$|⁠ . We follow the analyst forecasting literature and include controls for the logarithm of one plus overall experience of an analyst (measured in quarters), the logarithm of one plus the analyst’s firm-specific experience, and the logarithm of the age of the analyst forecast. We also control for the logarithm of the MSA-level gross domestic product (GDP) to account for the local economic conditions that analysts experience. 13 Additionally, we include firm level controls for the logarithm of the number of shares traded divided by the number of shares outstanding over the prior 12 months (log(Turnover)), the logarithm of the market capitalization of the firm (log(Size)), the logarithm of book value of equity minus the logarithm of market value of equity (Book to market), and the logarithm of the number of analysts covering a firm (log(Num analysts)). We further control for whether an analyst is located near the firm’s headquarters and whether the analyst is in an area with no local stores of the covered firm. We also include the logarithm of the average national car count in some specifications. We cluster standard errors at the MSA level. 14

We present the results of the effect of local signal of firm performance on individual analyst forecast error inTable 3. In column 1, we include|$Firm$|and|$YQ$|fixed effects to control for the possibility that some firms are more difficult to forecast than others as well as any potential time trends. We find that local firm performance is significantly associated with analyst forecast error. Specifically, a one-standard-deviation increase in the car count difference (0.46) is associated with a 36.8-bp increase in the forecast error, representing a 4.6|$\%$|deviation from the mean. We also find that national car count is significantly related to analyst forecasts, indicating that analysts are on average able to incorporate signals of a firm’s overall performance during the quarter.

In column 2, we introduce|${\it Firm} \times {\it YQ}$|fixed effects to control for any information about a firm that all analysts can access (e.g., company filings, earnings guidance calls with management) in a given quarter. All variation in firm-specific characteristics (turnover, size, book to market, and number of analysts) is subsumed under this model. The remaining specifications inTable 3are thus identified by variation in the local signal that analysts receive within the same firm-quarter, as proxied by|${\it Car\ Count}\ \mathit{Difference}$|⁠. The introduction of|${\it Firm} \times {\it YQ}$|fixed effects strengthens the relation between forecast error and|${\it Car\ Count}\ \mathit{Difference}$|⁠, as the coefficient for|${\it Car\ Count}\ \mathit{Difference}$|in column 2 increases to 0.0090. In column 3, we introduce|${\it MSA}$|fixed effects to examine variation in car count difference in a particular MSA for a firm. This model will control for any time-invariant measurement error that may arise from the satellite imagery in a certain location (e.g., incomplete coverage because of underground parking garages). The coefficient remains significant and similar in magnitude under this specification.

Given that parking lot car counts might be correlated within an MSA across firms, one remaining concern is that|${\it Car\ Count}\ \mathit{Difference}$|may simply proxy forgenerallocal economic performance in an area. Our final test inTable 3includes|${\it MSA} \times {\it YQ}$|fixed effects which allows us to rule out any general local economic conditions that may influence analyst forecasts. The coefficient for|${\it Car\ Count}\ \mathit{Difference}$|remains stable at 0.0097. As a check of this specification, we generate a bootstrapped sample distribution by randomly reassigning car count differences within an MSA. For each iteration, each observations for a firm-MSA-YQ is randomly assigned theCar Count Differenceof a different firm in the same MSA-YQ creating a counterfactualBootstrapped car count difference. We then reestimate the model fromTable 3, column 4. All other firm level characteristics remain the same. We repeat this process 10,000 times and report the distribution of the estimates ofBootstrapped car count differenceinFigure 3. The distribution is centered around zero suggesting that the MSA|$\times$|YQ fixed effects do a good job of eliminating any influence from local economic performance. The estimate from our main specification of 0.0097 is more than five standard deviations above the mean suggesting that our result is unlikely to occur by chance.15

3.2 Weight on local signals in the presence of other information

Next, we examine heterogeneity in analyst characteristics and firm information availability to investigate how an analyst’s weighting on local firm information may change based on the overall information environment. Local firm information may be more valuable to an analyst when fewer alternative sources of viable firm information are available to draw from. We test this conjecture in a number of ways. We hypothesize that analysts will likely have greater access to other sources of firm information when they have more experience, when there are more analysts covering the firm, and when there are analysts covering the firm from more MSAs. More experienced analysts likely have a better network from which to obtain firm information and also may be better suited to recognize noisy local firm information. There is also a greater opportunity to learn about overall firm information when more analysts are covering the firm and to learn about regional firm information when more analysts are covering the firm from other MSAs.

Beyond individual analyst characteristics, we test whether analysts rely less on local firm information when satellite data becomes commercially available, when firms release 8-K filings during the quarter, and when analysts make forecasts closer to the earnings announcement date. The availability of satellite data gives analysts the ability to see all parking lots and make their own local signal less valuable. Similarly, the release of SEC filings, such as 8-Ks, will give analysts more salient information and may make them less reliant on local firm performance. Finally, examining the timing of an analyst’s forecast within the quarter allows us to account most generally for other sources of information as an analyst will have access to a greater amount of information about the firm’s upcoming quarterly earnings as time passes.

We report results for these tests inTable 4. In the first three columns, we interact|${\it Car\ Count}\ \mathit{Difference}$|with analyst experience, the number of analysts covering the firm, and the number of analyst MSAs, respectively. The last three columns interact|${\it Car\ Count}\ \mathit{Difference}$|with an indicator for whether satellite data are available for a firm in that quarter, the number of 8Ks released during the preceding firm-quarter, and indicators for whether an analyst’s forecast was released early, in the middle, or late in the quarter (based on terciles of forecast age), respectively. For ease of interpretation, we z-score each continuous interaction term. We find evidence that more experienced analysts put less weight on local firm information. The coefficient for the interaction term suggests that a one-standard-deviation increase in experience is associated with a 23.5|$\%$|(0.0023/0.0098) decrease in local information incorporation relative to an analyst with average experience. We find no evidence that the raw number of analysts modulates the magnitude of the effect (column 2). However, we find the effect is mitigated when more analysts are in geographically diverse locations (column 3). This suggests that analysts only reduce the weighting of local information when other analystswho are not exposed to the same local informationare also covering the firm. One possibility is that when analysts are in the same MSA, the analyst can observe the other analysts’ forecasts that include the same local information. When their own local signal differs from analysts in other MSAs, their own local signal may conflict with the implied signal in the other forecasts, causing the analyst to put less weight on the signal. We explore the implications of this on the consensus forecast in Section4.

We test whether the availability of satellite data reduces local information weighting in column 4. The majority of our sample (78|$\%$|of observations) covers the period before satellite data was disseminated but the data for some companies did become available starting in mid-2014.16Therefore we are able to test whether the availability of satellite data affects local information weighting by interacting|${\it Car\ Count}\ \mathit{Difference}$|with an indicator variable identifying whether a firm’s satellite data are available from a satellite vendor.17The staggered introduction of the satellite data allows us to use firms that either had not had their data disseminated yet or never have their data released during our sample as a control group. Consistent with our conjecture, we find that analysts reduce, but do not eliminate, their weighting on local firm information for firms that have satellite data available.18Similarly, we find in column 5 that the availability of another source of aggregate firm information, 8-K filings, is associated with a significant reduction in analysts’ weighting on local firm information. Finally, in column 6 we find that forecasts issued earlier in the quarter rely more on local information, when less firmwide information is available. Specifically, the interaction of|${\it Car\ Count}\ \mathit{Difference}$|with the|${\it Early}$|indicator variable is 0.0137, while the interaction with the|${\it Late}$|indicator is 0.0068. Late forecasts made when more other information is available (including other analysts’ forecasts) put less weight on the local signal. The results shown inTable 4collectively show that the importance of local firm-specific information to an analyst is dependent on that analyst’s overall information set.

3.3 Robustness

Our next set of tests are robustness checks to ensure the validity of our results.O’Brien and Tan (2015)find that analysts are more likely to cover local firms than nonlocal ones and may provide more accurate forecasts (Bae, Stulz, and Tan 2008). To control for potential selection between analysts and the firms they cover, we include|${\it Analyst} \times {\it Firm}$|fixed effects inTable 5, column 1. These effects also control for time-invariant analyst characteristics, such as analyst proximity to a firm’s headquarters, an analyst’s personal connections (e.g., school ties) with firm management, analyst skill, and whether an analyst has industry-relevant experience.19We continue to find a strong effect under this model, with a coefficient for|${\it Car\ count}\ \mathit{difference}$|of 0.0127.

Jennings, Lee, and Matsumoto (2017)find that managers frequently refer to other firms in the same geographic location in earnings conference calls, suggesting the relevance of local information for analysts. In the second column ofTable 5, we add|${\it Analyst} \times {\it YQ}$|fixed effects to examine how local firm performance affects forecast error for the same analyst in the same quarter. This specification rules out the influence of any time-varying analyst-specific effects, including variations in an analyst’s mood because of weather conditions (Dehaan, Madsen, and Piotroski 2017) or the performance of local sports teams. The inclusion of|${\it Analyst} \times {\it YQ}$|fixed effects allows us to isolate local firm performance by exploiting variation in|${\it Car\ count}\ \mathit{difference}$|for the different firms that an analyst covers. The coefficient for|${\it Car\ count}\ \mathit{difference}$|is largest under this specification, with a coefficient of 0.0136, and is significant at the 1|$\%$|level. The overall increase in the size of the coefficient as our identification becomes more strict and is consistent with the idea that fixed effects control for the unobserved variables that would bias the results toward zero. For example, including|${\it Analyst} \times {\it Firm}$|fixed effects controls for the possibility that some analysts have special relationships with certain firms because of shared school ties (Cohen, Frazzini, and Malloy 2010) or past work experience (Bradley, Gokkaya, and Liu 2017), relationships that could potentially provide them with a more complete information set about the firm’s current performance. By controlling for these unobserved variables, we are able to isolate the effect of time-varying local firm-specific information and find a stronger effect.

Another concern is that given the industry concentration in New York, the results may be driven by some difference between New York and other metropolitan areas. To address this concern, in column 3 we estimate the model excluding New York-based analysts. We find consistent results, with a significant coefficient for|${\it Car\ Count}\ \mathit{Difference}$|of 0.008. Similarly, We test whether the effect may be concentrated in small (or large) MSAs by interacting|${\it Car\ Count}\ \mathit{Difference}$|with an indicator for MSAs with an above median geographic area of the MSA. The results, shown in column 4, show no significant difference in analyst weighting of local firm information based on the size of an MSA.20Because the satellites do not cover every MSA with the same frequency, the low number of parking lot observations in some MSAs during a quarter could create a spurious correlation. We address this potential concern in column 5 by excluding MSA-firm-quarters with fewer than five satellite observations and again find consistent results. Our last robustness check looks for any asymmetry in the influence of local performance. We check for monotonicity of the result by running a piecewise interactionCar count differenceover three tercile ranges (Low-Range, Mid-Range, and High-Range). Prior research on financial analysts has shown that negative signals can be more influential than positive signals (Hirshleifer et al. 2021). In column 6 ofTable 5, we find that each of interaction terms are positive and significant. Though the magnitude on the interaction with high- and low-range car counts is higher than for the mid-range, suggesting analysts may put extra weight on local information if the signals are significantly different from the typical signal, these differences are not statistically significant from each other.
article.4. Geographic Diversity, Consensus Earni:
In this section, we examine whether local information affects the consensus forecast error and firm liquidity. In a frictionless market, we would expect brokerage firms to address local information by locating a new analyst in an area that does not have an existing analyst covering the same firm. In this frictionless market, noise from local information would cancel out and we would see no effect on consensus forecast errors. However, several frictions prevent brokerage firms from adequately dispersing analysts. These frictions include the need to keep employees local in order to monitor them, the concentration of analyst talent, and the fact that brokerage firms typically only have one analyst cover each firm. Together, these frictions create an environment where firms have varying degrees of analyst geographic dispersion.

4.1 Satellite-retail and IBES samples

If forecasts are clustered in geographic areas based on the same local information, then we would expect the forecast errors to aggregate when analysts are clustered in the same MSA and to cancel out when they are disperse. Further, to the extent that diverse information can be obtained from different geographic areas, analyst geographic dispersion should improve a firm’s information environment (Goldstein and Yang 2015). Next, we turn to examining how the individual forecast errors interact at the consensus forecast level. We also test whether analysts’ geographic diversity is relevant for a stock’s bid-ask spread, a common proxy for the quality of a stock’s information environment. Since we can test these implications without using satellite data, in this section, we expand the sample to include all firms as we can measure the geographic dispersion using the intersection of the IBES and CRD databases.

As a simple test, we plot the relation between consensus forecast error (panel A) and bid-ask spread (panel B) by number of distinct MSAs covering a firm inFigure 4. Panel A provides suggestive evidence that when a firm is covered by analysts from more distinct MSAs the individual weightings on local information cancel out, producing lower consensus forecast errors. Likewise, panel B suggests that firm liquidity improves as analysts become more geographically dispersed, as a firm’s bid-ask spread around earnings announcements is lower when analysts are located in more distinct MSAs. However, a number of factors could confound such interpretations, so we proceed to estimate regression models to attempt to rule these potential confounders out.

To more formally test the relationship between consensus forecast error and analyst geographic dispersion, we estimate the following regression model: $$\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\textit{Consensus}\ \textit{Abs}(\textit{Error})_{j,q} = \beta_1 Y_{j,q} +
\sum\limits_{k}\beta_k \textit{Controls}_{j,q} + \Gamma \textit{FE} + \epsilon_{j,q}.
\end{split}
\end{equation}$$ (6)

We consider two measures of geographic dispersion for|$Y_{j,q}$|⁠. First, we create a Herfindahl index of the concentration of analysts in MSAs using the number of analysts covering a firm in each MSA. We multiply this Herfindahl index by negative one so that it may be interpreted as a dispersion index. Second, we use the number of distinct MSAs for which analysts covering the firm are located. If increased geographic dispersion is associated with lower consensus forecast errors, we would expect a positive|$\beta_1$|both for the dispersion index and for the number of MSAs. The specifications include controls forTurnover,Book to market,Size,Number of analysts,Firm age, and an indicator for highInstitutional ownership. Standard errors are clustered by firm.

Table 6reports tests of geographic dispersion on consensus analyst forecast error. Panel A reports results for the Orbital Insight sample, and panel B reports for the IBES-CRD sample. In both samples, we find evidence that consensus analyst forecast errors are lower when analysts are more dispersed (column 1) or in more MSAs (column 2). Importantly, column 2 also includes|$Number\ of\ Analysts$|fixed effects, holding constant the effect of the number of analysts on consensus forecast error, which provides compelling evidence that it is the location and not the amount of coverage that is driving the observed relationship.

Our next tests look for diminishing effects on geographic dispersion by including indicator variables in Equation (6) that identify the exact number of MSAs for each firm. We use firms whose analysts are all in one MSA as the baseline so that coefficients are interpreted relative to firms with the least geographically diverse analysts. We include the same controls as before as well as fixed effects forIndustry,21Number of Analysts, and theYQof the earnings announcement. We report the resultant coefficients and 95|$\%$|confidence intervals in panel C ofFigure 4. We find no evidence of diminishing effects of geographic dispersion, as the consensus forecast error monotonically declines as the number of MSAs increases.

Next, we test another financial market implication of analyst geographic diversity: information quality. Because analysts are one of the primary information intermediaries for stock valuation, shortcomings in their ability to convey all information about a firm could decrease the quality of the information available to investors. We test this idea using the following model: $$\begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\textit{Spread}_{j,q} = \beta_1 Y_{j,q} + \sum\limits_{k}\beta_k \textit{Controls}_{j,q}
+ \Gamma \textit{FE} + \epsilon_{j,q},
\end{split}
\end{equation}$$ (7) where |$Spread_{j,q}$| is the closing bid-ask spread scaled by the midpoint for the 3 days around the earnings announcement of firm |$j$| in quarter |$q$|⁠ . We again use the MSA dispersion index and the number of MSAs as our variables of interest. The results, shown in columns 3 and 4 of Table 6 , support the argument that analyst geographic diversity is positively related to an improvement in the information environment. We find spreads are lower when analysts are more dispersed as measured by the MSA dispersion index. We also find a reduction in spreads for firms that have analysts located in more MSAs, after controlling for the number of analysts. Specifically, a firm with one more MSA covered has a spread that is 1.73-bp (IBES sample) lower than an otherwise similar firm, representing a 7.2 |$\%$| reduction from the median spread of 24-bp. Panel D of Figure 4 shows that spreads reduce monotonically as the number of covered MSAs increase. We note that results across the Orbital Insight and IBES samples are similar, further supporting our claim that any differences between the samples are unlikely to diminish the generalizability of our results.

To ensure these analyses are indeed picking up a geographic diversity effect, we perform a few robustness checks. In part because of historical immigration patterns, surnames are geographically clustered in the United States.Merkley, Michaely, and Pacelli (2020)find relations between ethnic surname diversity among analysts covering a stock and consensus forecast errors. Therefore, we run a similar ethnic classification of analysts in our sample based on origin of their surnames (Ye et al. 2017). This process yields sensible patterns similar to well-documented overall national trends (e.g., we find that a disproportionate number of financial analysts have Irish surnames in the Boston area). We then replicate our main result inTable 6, panel B, using only observations in which analysts covering the stock at same time share a common surname origin (typically either Anglo-only or Germanic-only). We report this inTable A-5. We find our results are quite similar to the full sample results even though in this restricted sample ethnic diversity cannot play a role. We also examine geographic diversity at different levels of aggregation. As many of the top centers for equity research are in different regions (e.g., New York in the Northeast, Chicago in the Midwest, and San Francisco in the West), it is not surprising that geographic diversity measured using MSAs is highly correlated with those Census regions based measures.Table A-6reports the results using analyst dispersion based on the four U.S. census regions and finds similar results. Finally, we also look more broadly at the time trends of the effects of analyst geographic dispersion for all firms in our IBES sample inFigure A-1, where we plot coefficients of the interaction between the MSA dispersion index and year indicator variables. The model in panel A follows Equation (6), the model in panel B follows Equation (7). We find the average effect of analyst dispersion on forecast accuracy and liquidity has been largely persistent throughout time.22This is again consistent with the idea that other geographic information that is more difficult to quantify continues to be relevant even as big and alternative data becomes more prevalent.

We perform an industry subsample analysis inFigure 5in order to examine how widespread the effect of analyst geographic dispersion is. We report the coefficient for the MSA dispersion index from subsample regressions of the|$Consensus\ Forecast\ Error$|(panel A) and|$Spread$|(panel B) tests for each industry in the Fama-French 17 industry classification. We find the coefficient for the dispersion index is negative and statistically significant in the majority of industries. Although retail is among the most affected industries,Figure 5suggests the influence of analyst geographic dispersion is not solely concentrated in the retail industry.

4.2 Analyst geographic diversity and harder-to-value stocks

The benefits of analyst geographic dispersion are likely to be heterogeneous based on the value of geographic information for a firm.Bernile, Kumar, and Sulaeman (2015)show that investors local to firm operations have a particular information advantage for harder-to-value stocks. Based on their finding it is reasonable to expect that geographically diverse analysts will be particularly valuable for incorporating local information when stocks are more difficult to value. Motivated by this conjecture, we next test whether analyst geographic dispersion has a greater effect on consensus forecast error and firm liquidity for harder-to-value stocks.

To examine these heterogeneous effects, we interact our MSA dispersion index with firm characteristics that proxy for firm valuation difficulty. Following the literature, we identify hard-to-value firms as those that are smaller, younger, more R&D intensive, and have higher idiosyncratic volatility, skewness, and lower stock prices.23We measure firm size as the logarithm of market value, young as an indicator equal to one if the number of years since the firm’s initial public offering (IPO) is five or fewer,24idiosyncratic volatility as the value-weighted standard deviation of residuals from regressions of firm return on market return using the past month of daily data, skewness as the skewness of daily returns over the previous month,25and R&D as quarterly R&D expenses scaled by the book value of assets at the end of the previous quarter.26We normalize each continuous variable to have a mean of zero and standard deviation of one. We also multiply firm size and stock price by negative one so that the interaction terms may be interpreted as increases in the difficulty of valuing the stock.

We report results of the cross-sectional tests inTable 7. The results are consistent with our intuition, as the MSA dispersion index is more negatively associated with consensus errors for firms that are smaller, that are younger, have lower stock prices, have higher idiosyncratic volatility, and have higher skewness (panel A). We find similar effects in panel B when using firm liquidity as the dependent variable and also find stronger effects on liquidity for more R&D-intensive firms. The coefficients for the interaction terms indicate that analyst geographic dispersion is particularly beneficial for smaller and lower priced firms. Importantly, the effects of analyst geographic dispersion are still significant both economically and statistically for the average firm as the coefficient for the MSA dispersion index is negative and significant for all specifications. Overall,Table 7supports the idea that analyst geographic dispersion is more beneficial when the incorporation of local information is relatively more valuable.

4.3 Changes in analyst geographic dispersion

Our next objective is to further identify the analyst geographic diversity effect and attempt to rule out unobserved confounders. To do so, we revisit Equations (6) and (7) and include|$Firm$|fixed effects. We continue to find a negative|$\beta_1$|on the MSA dispersion index (Table 8, columns 1 and 4) and the number of MSAs (columns 2 and 5) even within the same firm. Because the variation is within-firm, the effect is being estimated off changes in geographic coverage decisions of brokerage houses. While the choice of an individual analyst to cover a firm could plausibly be affected by the information environment of the firm, it is less clear why the geographic dispersion of coverage would be similarly affected (controlling for the number of analysts).

We have no obvious reason to believe that endogeneity would be an important concern in this setting. Brokerage firms are unlikely to shift their entire research staff’s operations in response to their relative geographic dispersion for a single stock. However, the exogenous shift in analyst coverage due to brokerage closures and mergers is useful in our setting as it allows us to rule out any uncertainty regarding endogeneity. We followHong and Kacperczyk (2010)andKelly and Ljungqvist (2012)to identify exogenous drops in coverage and look within firms that lose coverage due to the exogenous reduction in analyst coverage. The reductions in analyst coverage are either the result of the closure of a brokerage’s entire research operation or the result of a brokerage-firm merger where both firms employed analysts covering the same firm before the merger and only one analyst covers the firm after the merger. While prior research has used this setting as a shock to competition and information asymmetry, we are interested in the shift in analyst geographic diversity due to the brokerage closures and mergers. For example, a stock that loses coverage from its only analyst located in Minneapolis will lose geographic diversity, while a stock that loses coverage from one of three analysts located in New York will retain the same level of geographic diversity. In our sample, about one-third (2,355 of 6,550) of the cases of a loss of coverage also have a reduction in the number of covered MSAs. Geographic coverage for any particular firm is unlikely to be an important determination in whether an analyst is terminated; any analyst could be simultaneously a geographically unique analyst for one firm and a geographically common analyst for another. Further, this exogenous shift will only affect analyst forecast accuracy and the information environment through the change in geographic dispersion and will be uninformative about stocks’ future performance.

This setting also allows us to test a prediction related to theory on information diversity and market uncertainty. Specifically,Goldstein and Yang (2015)suggest using this setting to identify exogenous changes in diverse analyst information to see whether a reduction in information production affects market uncertainty. Our innovation is identifying geographically specific information around analysts as a source of diverse information and empirically testing whether a reduction in access to that information affects consensus forecasts and firm liquidity. An increase in consensus forecast error for firms that lose geographic diversity relative to those that retain the same geographic diversity would lend even more support to the idea that analyst geographic dispersion is an important determinant of analyst forecast accuracy. An increase in the bid-ask spread for firms that lose a geographically diverse analyst would suggest geographic diversity is one channel through which analysts can improve a stock’s information environment.

We show the results for the exogenous change in analyst geographic dispersion in columns 3 and 6 ofTable 8. We find larger effects on both consensus error and spread in those firms that see reduced geographic coverage (e.g., a firm loses its only analyst who is located in Minneapolis) relative to firms that also lose analyst coverage but retain the same geographic diversity (e.g., a firm with three analysts in the New York city metropolitan area loses one of the three New York analysts). This finding provides causal evidence that analyst geographic dispersion increases consensus forecast quality and improves the information environment, consistent with the model implications ofGoldstein and Yang (2015).
article.6. Conclusion:
Exploiting novel data from satellite images of parking lots of U.S. retailers, we demonstrate that analysts shade their forecast in the direction of the local car counts relative to other analysts covering the same firm from different locations. Research analysts appear to rely on local firm-specific information in the production of their forecasts and rely more on these local signals when less firmwide information is available.

The influence of this local information and diversity of local sources affects the overall consensus forecast error and firm liquidity. When firms have more geographically diverse coverage the consensus forecast error is lower. Bid-ask spreads are also reduced for firms with dispersed analysts, suggesting that analyst geographic diversity has important implications for market liquidity. Analyst geographic dispersion is even more important when stocks are relatively hard to value. We provide evidence that these effects are causal using within-firm analyses and exogenous shocks to coverage due to brokerage closures. We also show that the market values analyst geographic diversity. Market reactions to analyst revisions are stronger when analysts are located in unique MSAs and prices are more informative when analysts are spread out geographically.

To date, research on geography in financial markets has primarily focused on the importance of being close to a firm’s headquarters for access to information. We document a rich relationship between geographic diversity of analysts in the production of equity research. Our findings also highlight a key driver regarding the wisdom of crowds. By identifying local firm-specific performance as a source of private information, we show that incorporating more diverse private information can lead to an improvement in accuracy. In other words, there is value in differing points of view.
